_meta.note: AI-authored to high-confidence board-standard anesthesia pharmacology facts; awaits credentialed review before status='published'.

questions.subtopic: Volatile agents — factors that decrease MAC
questions.stem: Several patient and drug factors lower the minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of a volatile anesthetic. Which of the following DECREASES MAC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concurrent opioid administration
choices.rationale: Opioids, like other CNS depressants, lower anesthetic requirement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic alcohol use
choices.rationale: Chronic alcohol use tends to increase MAC via cross-tolerance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperthermia
choices.rationale: Increased temperature raises MAC; hypothermia lowers it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute amphetamine intoxication
choices.rationale: Acute sympathomimetic intoxication raises MAC.

questions.explanation: MAC is decreased by advancing age, hypothermia, pregnancy, opioids and other CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alpha-2 agonists), acute alcohol intoxication, hyponatremia, and hypoxemia/hypotension. MAC is increased by hyperthermia, chronic alcohol use, acute sympathomimetic use (amphetamines, cocaine), and red hair. Opioids are a classic MAC-sparing adjunct.

questions.stem: Immediately after discontinuing a high-concentration nitrous oxide anesthetic, a patient breathing room air becomes transiently hypoxemic. What is the mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid outpouring of nitrous oxide into the alveoli dilutes alveolar oxygen (diffusion hypoxia)
choices.rationale: Large volumes of N2O leaving the blood dilute alveolar O2 and CO2 on emergence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nitrous oxide directly binds hemoglobin and blocks oxygen carriage
choices.rationale: N2O does not bind hemoglobin in this way.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Methemoglobin formation from nitrous oxide
choices.rationale: N2O does not cause methemoglobinemia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bronchospasm triggered by nitrous oxide
choices.rationale: N2O is not a bronchospastic trigger.

questions.explanation: Diffusion hypoxia occurs at the end of a nitrous oxide anesthetic: because N2O is relatively insoluble, large volumes rapidly leave the blood and flood the alveoli, diluting alveolar oxygen (and CO2). Administering 100% oxygen for a few minutes during emergence prevents it.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: A critically ill patient on a prolonged high-dose propofol infusion develops metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, and cardiac dysfunction. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS)
choices.rationale: Prolonged high-dose propofol impairs mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation, producing this constellation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: MH is triggered by volatile agents/succinylcholine, not propofol, and presents with hypercarbia/rigidity intraoperatively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: LAST follows local anesthetic dosing, not propofol infusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serotonin syndrome
choices.rationale: Serotonin syndrome involves serotonergic drugs and neuromuscular hyperactivity, not this metabolic picture.

questions.explanation: Propofol infusion syndrome is a rare, potentially fatal complication of prolonged (>48 h) high-dose (>4 mg/kg/h) propofol, especially in critically ill or pediatric patients. It reflects impaired mitochondrial fatty-acid metabolism: metabolic (lactic) acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, hepatomegaly, and refractory bradycardia/cardiac failure. Management is to stop propofol and provide supportive care.

questions.stem: Ketamine is chosen for induction in a hemodynamically unstable trauma patient with reactive airway disease. Which statement about ketamine is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is an NMDA-receptor antagonist that preserves airway reflexes, causes bronchodilation, and generally increases heart rate and blood pressure
choices.rationale: Ketamine's dissociative anesthesia and indirect sympathomimetic effects make it useful in shock and bronchospasm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a potent myocardial depressant that lowers blood pressure
choices.rationale: Ketamine usually raises blood pressure via sympathetic stimulation (though it is a direct myocardial depressant unmasked in catecholamine-depleted patients).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It reliably suppresses airway reflexes and causes apnea like propofol
choices.rationale: Ketamine tends to preserve airway reflexes and respiratory drive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a GABA-A agonist
choices.rationale: Ketamine acts mainly at the NMDA receptor, not GABA-A.

questions.explanation: Ketamine produces dissociative anesthesia via NMDA-receptor antagonism. It indirectly stimulates the sympathetic nervous system, typically increasing heart rate and blood pressure (useful in hypovolemic shock), and it is a bronchodilator (useful in asthma). Caveat: in catecholamine-depleted patients its direct myocardial depressant effect can be unmasked, causing hypotension.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: Etomidate is valued for hemodynamic stability on induction. What is a recognized adverse effect even after a single induction dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transient adrenocortical suppression via inhibition of 11-beta-hydroxylase
choices.rationale: Etomidate inhibits cortisol synthesis; even a single dose causes measurable, transient adrenal suppression.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Profound and sustained hypotension
choices.rationale: Etomidate is notable for hemodynamic stability, not hypotension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: Etomidate is not an MH trigger.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Histamine release causing bronchospasm
choices.rationale: Etomidate does not cause significant histamine release.

questions.explanation: Etomidate provides cardiovascular stability on induction but inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase, suppressing cortisol synthesis. Even a single induction dose causes transient adrenal suppression; continuous infusion is avoided, and caution is advised in septic/critically ill patients. Other effects include myoclonus and injection pain.

questions.stem: After a single dose of succinylcholine, a patient remains paralyzed and apneic far longer than expected. Pseudocholinesterase (butyrylcholinesterase) deficiency is suspected. What is the appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue mechanical ventilation and sedation until neuromuscular function recovers
choices.rationale: There is no specific reversal; supportive ventilation until the prolonged block resolves is the standard of care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give neostigmine to reverse the depolarizing block
choices.rationale: Neostigmine does not reliably reverse a depolarizing (phase I) block and can worsen it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer dantrolene
choices.rationale: Dantrolene treats malignant hyperthermia, not prolonged succinylcholine block.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give additional succinylcholine to overcome the block
choices.rationale: More succinylcholine prolongs paralysis and risks a phase II block.

questions.explanation: Atypical or deficient pseudocholinesterase slows succinylcholine metabolism, causing prolonged paralysis (dibucaine number identifies atypical enzyme). There is no antidote: keep the patient sedated and ventilated until neuromuscular function returns (confirmed by a nerve stimulator). Counsel the patient and family for future anesthetics.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: A patient with both significant renal and hepatic failure needs nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade. Which agent is most appropriate because its elimination is independent of organ function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cisatracurium
choices.rationale: Cisatracurium undergoes organ-independent Hofmann elimination (temperature/pH dependent).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rocuronium
choices.rationale: Rocuronium relies substantially on hepatic (biliary) elimination and is prolonged in hepatic failure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vecuronium
choices.rationale: Vecuronium is hepatically and renally eliminated and accumulates in organ failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pancuronium
choices.rationale: Pancuronium is largely renally excreted and markedly prolonged in renal failure.

questions.explanation: Cisatracurium is eliminated by Hofmann elimination, a spontaneous, pH- and temperature-dependent breakdown that is independent of hepatic and renal function, making it ideal in combined organ failure. (Unlike atracurium, the lower cisatracurium dose produces little laudanosine and minimal histamine release.)

references.source: Miller's Anesthesia, 9th ed., neuromuscular blocking drugs

questions.stem: Why should morphine be used cautiously in a patient with renal failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide can cause prolonged sedation and respiratory depression
choices.rationale: M6G is renally cleared and active; it accumulates in renal failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Morphine is directly nephrotoxic
choices.rationale: Morphine is not primarily nephrotoxic; the issue is metabolite accumulation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal failure speeds morphine metabolism, reducing efficacy
choices.rationale: Renal failure slows clearance of the active metabolite, increasing effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Morphine chelates calcium causing hypocalcemia
choices.rationale: This is not a property of morphine.

questions.explanation: Morphine is metabolized to morphine-3-glucuronide (inactive) and morphine-6-glucuronide (a potent active opioid). M6G depends on renal clearance and accumulates in renal failure, causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression. Fentanyl or hydromorphone are often preferred, with careful titration.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: Dexmedetomidine is selected for sedation during an awake fiberoptic intubation. Which property makes it well suited to this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides sedation and analgesia with minimal respiratory depression
choices.rationale: As a central alpha-2 agonist, it sedates while largely preserving respiratory drive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a profound respiratory depressant requiring controlled ventilation
choices.rationale: Minimal respiratory depression is precisely its advantage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It reliably raises heart rate and blood pressure
choices.rationale: It typically causes bradycardia and hypotension (a loading-dose bolus can transiently raise BP).

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is an NMDA antagonist
choices.rationale: It is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist, not an NMDA antagonist.

questions.explanation: Dexmedetomidine is a selective central alpha-2 agonist producing sedation, anxiolysis, and analgesia with minimal respiratory depression — ideal for awake airway management and ICU sedation. Expect bradycardia and hypotension; a rapid loading bolus can cause transient hypertension via peripheral alpha-2B stimulation.

questions.subtopic: Local anesthetics — ester vs amide allergy
questions.stem: A patient reports a true allergy to a local anesthetic. Which statement about local anesthetic allergy is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: True allergy is far more common with ester local anesthetics, whose metabolism produces PABA
choices.rationale: Esters are hydrolyzed to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), a recognized allergen; amide allergy is rare.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amide local anesthetics are the usual cause of true allergy
choices.rationale: True amide allergy is rare; preservatives may be implicated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Local anesthetic allergy never cross-reacts within a class
choices.rationale: Cross-reactivity can occur within the ester class via the common PABA metabolite.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All reported reactions are true IgE-mediated allergy
choices.rationale: Many 'reactions' are vasovagal or due to intravascular epinephrine, not true allergy.

questions.explanation: True local anesthetic allergy is uncommon and is much more often associated with ester agents (procaine, tetracaine, benzocaine, chloroprocaine), which are metabolized to PABA, a known allergen. Amide allergy is rare; reported reactions are frequently due to additives/preservatives (e.g., methylparaben) or systemic epinephrine. When in doubt, a preservative-free amide is generally safe.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: Compared with atropine, why is glycopyrrolate often preferred as the antimuscarinic given with neostigmine for neuromuscular reversal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycopyrrolate is a quaternary amine that does not cross the blood-brain barrier, avoiding central anticholinergic effects
choices.rationale: Its quaternary structure limits CNS entry, avoiding central anticholinergic syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glycopyrrolate crosses the placenta more readily, protecting the fetus
choices.rationale: Quaternary glycopyrrolate crosses the placenta poorly; this is not the rationale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycopyrrolate has a faster onset that matches atropine exactly
choices.rationale: Glycopyrrolate has a slower onset than atropine; the match is with neostigmine's slower muscarinic onset.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atropine cannot treat bradycardia
choices.rationale: Atropine treats bradycardia well; the distinction is CNS penetration.

questions.explanation: Glycopyrrolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic that does not cross the blood-brain barrier, so it avoids central anticholinergic syndrome (confusion, sedation) seen with the tertiary amine atropine, which does cross. Glycopyrrolate's slower onset also better matches neostigmine's muscarinic time course, smoothing reversal.

questions.stem: Which property makes esmolol useful for treating brief intraoperative tachycardia or hypertension (e.g., at laryngoscopy)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid hydrolysis by red-blood-cell esterases gives it a very short half-life (~9 minutes)
choices.rationale: Esterase metabolism makes its effect brief and titratable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a long-acting, irreversible beta antagonist
choices.rationale: Esmolol is ultra-short acting, the opposite of long-acting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a nonselective beta and alpha blocker like labetalol
choices.rationale: Esmolol is a cardioselective beta-1 antagonist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depends on hepatic CYP metabolism
choices.rationale: It is metabolized by blood esterases, not the liver.

questions.explanation: Esmolol is a cardioselective (beta-1) antagonist rapidly hydrolyzed by red-cell esterases, giving a half-life of about 9 minutes. Its quick on/off profile makes it ideal for transient stimuli such as laryngoscopy, with effects that dissipate quickly if bradycardia or hypotension occur.

references.source: Stoelting's Pharmacology & Physiology in Anesthetic Practice

questions.stem: At the end of a case the train-of-four shows no twitches (deep block). Why is neostigmine alone an inappropriate choice to reverse at this depth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neostigmine has a ceiling effect and cannot reliably reverse deep block; adequate spontaneous recovery (e.g., several twitches) must be present
choices.rationale: Acetylcholinesterase inhibition saturates; deep block needs more recovery or sugammadex.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Neostigmine deepens any block regardless of depth
choices.rationale: Neostigmine reverses shallow block; it simply cannot reverse profound block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neostigmine only works on depolarizing blockade
choices.rationale: Neostigmine reverses nondepolarizing block; it does not reverse depolarizing block.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neostigmine must be given without an antimuscarinic
choices.rationale: It is given WITH an antimuscarinic (glycopyrrolate/atropine) to limit muscarinic effects.

questions.explanation: Neostigmine reverses nondepolarizing blockade by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, but it has a ceiling effect: once the enzyme is maximally inhibited, no further reversal is possible. Deep block (no train-of-four twitches) should not be reversed with neostigmine alone — wait for spontaneous recovery to at least 2-4 twitches, or use sugammadex for aminosteroid agents.

references.source: Miller's Anesthesia, 9th ed., neuromuscular blocking drugs

questions.stem: Why is bupivacaine considered more cardiotoxic than lidocaine at equipotent doses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It binds cardiac sodium channels avidly and dissociates slowly ('fast-in, slow-out'), promoting refractory arrhythmias
choices.rationale: Slow dissociation from cardiac Na+ channels underlies bupivacaine's arrhythmogenicity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has no effect on cardiac sodium channels
choices.rationale: Its strong, prolonged sodium-channel binding is the mechanism of toxicity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is metabolized to a cardiotoxic methemoglobin-forming compound
choices.rationale: Methemoglobinemia is associated with prilocaine/benzocaine, not bupivacaine cardiotoxicity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It selectively blocks potassium channels only
choices.rationale: The dominant mechanism is sodium-channel binding.

questions.explanation: Bupivacaine binds cardiac sodium channels strongly and dissociates slowly during diastole ('fast-in, slow-out'), so toxicity can manifest as sudden, refractory ventricular arrhythmias and cardiac arrest — sometimes before or without classic CNS warning signs. This is why ropivacaine and levobupivacaine (less cardiotoxic) are often chosen, and why lipid emulsion is first-line for LAST.

questions.stem: A patient at high risk for postoperative nausea is to receive ondansetron. Which safety consideration is most relevant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ondansetron can prolong the QT interval; use caution with other QT-prolonging drugs or in patients with long QT
choices.rationale: 5-HT3 antagonists, including ondansetron, are associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ondansetron commonly causes extrapyramidal symptoms like metoclopramide
choices.rationale: EPS are associated with dopamine antagonists (metoclopramide), not 5-HT3 antagonists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ondansetron is a potent respiratory depressant
choices.rationale: Ondansetron does not cause respiratory depression.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ondansetron must never be combined with dexamethasone
choices.rationale: Ondansetron is frequently combined with dexamethasone for multimodal PONV prophylaxis.

questions.explanation: Ondansetron, a 5-HT3 antagonist, is first-line for PONV but causes dose-dependent QT prolongation; avoid or monitor in patients with congenital long QT, electrolyte abnormalities, or on other QT-prolonging agents. It is commonly and safely combined with dexamethasone as multimodal prophylaxis.

references.source: Miller's Anesthesia, 9th ed.; ASA/SAMBA PONV guidelines
